title: Lesson 200
transcript: Thank you. As part of the risk management process, we talked about how there was three major stages to risk management, value, risk, and treatment. Let's dig into treatment in a little more detail. Let's talk about how we go about treating risk. And as we talked about just briefly before, there's four major ways that we can treat risk. avoid, transfer, mitigate, and accept. So let's look at each of these in a little more detail, and I have to apologize for my horrible PowerPoint drawing skills here, but this diagram is meant to show a diving board with two different levels, and a pool of water that you're somehow physically supposed to be able to land in even though the diving board doesn't line up. So just ignore my terrible drawing skills and assume that somehow this would physically work. But let's use this diving board as a method of thinking about the four different treatment methods. And the first method means that we avoid the risk. So what does risk avoidance mean? When we say risk avoidance, what are we truly doing here? Well, risk avoidance is you don't do whatever the risky thing is, you avoid the risk. So if moving to the cloud is super risky, the way that you would avoid that risk is, not move to the cloud. But there's a really big downside to risk avoidance, and that is opportunity cost. If you avoid a risk, you avoid the upside of what that risk might have gotten you. And there's a lot of advantages to moving to the cloud as an example. And so if you entirely avoid the cloud, you avoid all of those potential advantages. And so that's a big downside with risk avoidance. And you can see this in real life. If an organization is too risk adverse, if they don't take any risks, what's inevitably going to happen to that organization? they will cease to exist. They won't continue on in the marketplace anymore. You always have to take a measured degree of risk. So risk avoidance is good for the really, really sketchy, scary things, but not good for everything. So what's the next way that we can treat risk? Well, oh, and by the way, how would we depict risk avoidance using the diver board? Go the other way. Don't jump. That's how you avoid the risk. Now, what's the next way that we can treat risk? Transfer. And using the diving board analogy here, transfer the risk would maybe you get someone else to jump. I don't know. See if they make it. But you should actually associate transference with one word. And that word is insurance. The way that we transfer the risk to someone else is to buy insurance. But keep in mind two very important words that we've already talked about. Accountability and risk. responsibility, because it's very important to understand that if an organization, for instance, has the risk in that they have to protect important data because they're the owners of that data, can they transfer the accountability for the protection of that data? And the answer is emphatically, absolutely no. They can transfer the responsibility but not the accountability. Now, what's the next way that we can treat risk? And this is frankly where we spend the vast majority of our time in security, and that is mitigation. And fundamentally what mitigation means is we put in place controls that try and reduce the risk down to an acceptable level. But can we ever reduce the risk down to zero? The answer is no. We can never find the perfect set of controls that will reduce risk to zero. So that's where I'll introduce a new term here. When you've reduced the risk as much as you can, there will always be what is known as some residual risk left over. And so using the diving board analogy here, how would we try and mitigate or reduce the risk? Well, we could try jumping off the lower diving board and maybe that would somehow survive that. Now, what's the final way that we can treat risk? Acceptance. Acceptance simply means we accept the risk. And there's a few places that we would do this. So I just talked about mitigation and how we always have some residual risk left over. That risk is typically accepted. We might also decide to accept risk if the cost of the controls exceeds the cost of the risk occurring. In other words, the controls are not cost justified. In that case, we might just decide to accept the risk. Now, the final really important thing to think about with risk acceptance is who should be accepting the risk. And the answer is the owner. The owner is always the one that should be accepting the risk for a particular asset. Why the owner? Well, because they best understand exactly what that asset is, how valuable it is, how big of a deal would it be to the organization if that risk occurred. They're best positioned to understand all that. and therefore they're best positioned to accept the risk. And using the final little diving board analogy here, here's how we could accept risk. Just jump from the top. So that's risk treatment. Thank you.
